docs.title: CDMConnector (Python)
docs.text: CDMConnector is a Python port of the R CDMConnector and omopgenerics packages. It provides tools for working with observational health data in the OMOP Common Data Model format using Ibis for lazy SQL pushdown (replacing dplyr/dbplyr in R).

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Cdm: A single object holding OMOP table references (Ibis tables) plus metadata (name, version, schemas).
  • CdmSource / DbCdmSource: Backed by an Ibis connection (DuckDB, Postgres, etc.) with write-schema support.
  • Cohort tables: OMOP cohort structure (cohort_definition_id, subject_id, cohort_start_date, cohort_end_date), attrition, cohort counts.
  • Eunomia: Helpers to download and use example OMOP datasets (e.g. GiBleed).
  • Schema-aware: cdm_schema, write_schema, optional achilles_schema; works with Ibis backends.

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
pip install CDMConnector

With optional backends:

pip install "CDMConnector[postgres]"
pip install "CDMConnector[dev,docs]"

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
import cdmconnector as cdm
import ibis

# From an Ibis DuckDB connection
con = ibis.duckdb.connect("path/to/cdm.duckdb")
cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(
    con,
    cdm_schema="main",
    write_schema="main",
    cdm_name="My CDM",
)

# Access tables (Ibis expressions)
person = cdm_ref.person
person = cdm_ref["person"]

# Lazy query (Ibis)
result = (
    cdm_ref.person
    .filter(cdm_ref.person.year_of_birth >= 1990)
    .select("person_id", "year_of_birth")
)
# Execute: result.execute() or materialize with cdm.compute(...)

docs.title: License
docs.text: Apache 2.0.

docs.title: Release checklist (PyPI)
docs.text:
Before publishing to PyPI:

  1. Version

  2. Bump version in pyproject.toml and src/cdmconnector/__init__.py (or use single source from pyproject).

  3. Tests

  4. Run: pip install -e ".[dev]" && pytest tests -v

  5. Ensure Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12 (CI covers these).

  6. Lint

  7. Run: ruff check src tests

  8. Fix any reported issues.

  9. Docs

  10. Run: mkdocs build

  11. Confirm no broken links or missing API pages.

  12. Build

  13. pip install build
  14. python -m build

  15. Check dist/ for .whl and .tar.gz.

  16. Publish

  17. pip install twine

  18. twine upload dist/* (use Test PyPI first: twine upload --repository testpypi dist/*).

  19. Changelog

  20. Update CHANGELOG.md with the release date and any last-minute notes.

Optional: Add a [tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel] exclude for tests/docs if needed; current config only packages src/cdmconnector.

docs.title: Working with cohorts
docs.text: Cohorts are a fundamental building block for observational health data analysis. A cohort is a set of persons satisfying one or more inclusion criteria for a duration of time. In set terms, a cohort can be represented as a set of person-days. In the OMOP Common Data Model, cohorts are stored in tables with four required columns.

docs.title: Cohort table structure
docs.text:
cohort_definition_id subject_id cohort_start_date cohort_end_date 1 1000 2020-01-01 2020-05-01 1 2000 2020-03-01 2020-09-01 2 1000 2020-02-01 2020-03-01

A cohort table can contain multiple cohorts; each cohort can have multiple persons. There can be multiple records per person in a single cohort as long as date ranges do not overlap. For more on cohorts in OHDSI, see The Book of OHDSI – Cohorts.

Cohort metadata is stored in related tables: *_set (cohort definitions/names), *_attrition (attrition steps).

docs.title: Cohort generation
docs.text:
The cohort table is created through cohort generation: combining a cohort definition with a CDM to produce a cohort table. The OMOP CDM standard allows the same cohort definition to be run on any OMOP CDM dataset.

Cohort definitions can be created in several ways:

  1. Atlas – Web application with a graphical cohort builder (Atlas demo, EHDEN Academy).
  2. Capr (R) – Code-based cohort definitions in R.
  3. Custom SQL or Python – CDMConnector provides generate_concept_cohort_set for simple concept-based cohorts.

docs.title: Atlas cohort definitions
docs.text:
Atlas cohorts are stored as JSON files. Use read_cohort_set to load a folder of cohort JSON files (and optional CohortsToCreate.csv), then generate_cohort_set to generate them on the CDM:

import cdmconnector as cdm
import ibis

path_to_cohort_jsons = "path/to/cohorts_folder"  # or use packaged example data
cohort_set = cdm.read_cohort_set(path_to_cohort_jsons)

con = ibis.duckdb.connect(cdm.eunomia_dir("GiBleed"))
cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(con, cdm_schema="main", write_schema="main", cdm_name="eunomia")

cdm_ref = cdm.generate_cohort_set(cdm_ref, cohort_set, name="study_cohorts")
# cdm_ref["study_cohorts"] is the generated cohort table

The generated cohort has associated metadata tables; use cohort_count, attrition, and settings (or the cohort’s cohort_set / cohort_attrition attributes) to inspect counts and attrition.

docs.title: Cohort characteristics
docs.text:
To summarise characteristics of cohorts (counts, demographics such as age, sex, prior/future observation), use summarise_characteristics or summarise_cohort_count (counts only). Results are returned in summarised result format; use table_characteristics to format them into a table.

import cdmconnector as cdm

# cohort: Ibis table or DataFrame with cohort_definition_id, subject_id, cohort_start_date, cohort_end_date
# cdm: optional Cdm (for demographics and cohort names)
result = cdm.summarise_characteristics(cohort, cdm=cdm, counts=True, demographics=True)
# result.results, result.settings

counts_only = cdm.summarise_cohort_count(cohort, cdm=cdm)
table = cdm.table_characteristics(result)

See API: characteristics for full reference.

docs.title: Concept-based cohorts
docs.text:
For simple cohorts based on OMOP concept IDs, use generate_concept_cohort_set:

cdm_ref = cdm.generate_concept_cohort_set(
    cdm_ref,
    concept_set={"gibleed": [192671]},
    name="gibleed_cohort",
    limit="all",
    end_days=10,
)

You can then filter or refine the cohort with Ibis and record attrition with record_cohort_attrition.

docs.title: Subsetting a cohort
docs.text:
To create a new cohort that is a subset of an existing one (e.g. only persons with cohort duration ≥ 28 days), build an Ibis query, persist it with compute, and wrap it as a cohort table with the appropriate metadata:

from cdmconnector import cdm
from cdmconnector.dates import datediff

# Filter to persons in cohort at least 28 days
subset_expr = (
    cdm_ref["study_cohorts"]
    .filter(datediff("cohort_start_date", "cohort_end_date") >= 28)
)
cdm_ref["cohort_subset"] = cdm.compute(subset_expr, name="cohort_subset", temporary=False, overwrite=True)
# Then attach cohort_set and cohort_attrition (e.g. via new_cohort_table or your package’s helpers)

docs.title: Custom cohort from a table
docs.text: If you have a table in the CDM (or write schema) with the four required columns, you can add it to the CDM and use it like any other cohort. Ensure the table has cohort_definition_id, subject_id, cohort_start_date, and cohort_end_date. Use insert_table to add it to the CDM, then attach cohort set and attrition metadata as required by your workflow.

docs.title: Cohort counts
docs.text:
cohort_count(cohort) returns the latest counts per cohort_definition_id from the cohort’s attrition table (if present):

import cdmconnector as cdm

counts = cdm.cohort_count(cohort)

docs.title: Attrition
docs.text:
attrition(cohort) returns the full attrition table (all steps):

attr = cdm.attrition(cohort)

docs.title: Recording attrition
docs.text: record_cohort_attrition(cohort, reason, cohort_id=None) adds a new attrition step. If cohort_id is omitted, all cohort definitions are updated. Use this when you filter or modify a cohort and want to document the step.

docs.title: New cohort table
docs.text:
new_cohort_table(cdm, name, overwrite=True) creates an empty cohort table in the CDM’s write schema, plus name_set and name_attrition, and adds the table to the CDM:

cdm_ref = cdm.new_cohort_table(cdm_ref, "my_cohort", overwrite=True)

Requires a database-backed CDM (cdm_from_con).

docs.title: Combining cohort tables
docs.text:
bind combines multiple cohort tables into one, as long as cohort names are unique across the set:

combined = cdm.bind(cdm_ref["cohort_a"], cdm_ref["cohort_b"], name="combined_cohorts")

docs.title: Database backends
docs.text: CDMConnector uses Ibis for all table and query operations. Supported backends are those supported by Ibis (DuckDB, Postgres, Snowflake, BigQuery, Spark, etc.).

docs.title: Schema handling
docs.text:
• cdm_schema: Schema (Ibis “database”) where CDM tables live.
  • write_schema: Schema for cohort and other tables you create.
  • achilles_schema: Optional schema for Achilles tables.

For DuckDB, the default schema is main. You can pass cdm_schema="main" and write_schema="main" when they are the same.

For backends with catalog + schema (e.g. Snowflake), pass a dict, e.g. {"catalog": "my_catalog", "schema": "my_schema"}.

docs.title: Write prefix
docs.text: cdm_from_con(..., write_prefix="tmp_") prefixes all tables created in the write schema with tmp_, so you can avoid name clashes in a shared schema.

docs.title: Source operations
docs.text:
• list_source_tables(cdm): Tables in the write schema.
  • read_source_table(cdm, name): Ibis table for a table in the write schema.
  • insert_table(cdm, name, table, overwrite=True): Insert an Ibis expression or Arrow/pandas table into the write schema; adds it to the CDM.
  • drop_table(cdm, name): Drop one or more tables from the write schema and remove them from the CDM.

All of these require a database-backed CDM (cdm_from_con).

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
This tutorial is for new users who have access to data already mapped into the OMOP Common Data Model (CDM) format. CDMConnector also provides synthetic example datasets in OMOP CDM format. To learn more about the OMOP CDM or the mapping process, see:

  • EHDEN Academy
  • The Book of OHDSI
  • OHDSI Community
  • Common Data Model

docs.title: Creating a reference to the OMOP CDM
docs.text:
OMOP CDM datasets are typically stored in a database and can range from hundreds of patients to hundreds of millions. The OHDSI community supports Postgres, SQL Server, Oracle, and cloud platforms such as Amazon Redshift, Google Big Query, Databricks, and Snowflake. The first step is to create a connection to your database. See the Connection examples tutorial for backend-specific setup.

In the examples below we use synthetic data from the Synthea project mapped to OMOP CDM format, with DuckDB as a file-based database. To list available example datasets, use example_datasets().

docs.title: Connect to a CDM (DuckDB)
docs.text:
Use an Ibis DuckDB connection and cdm_from_con:

import cdmconnector as cdm
import ibis

# List example datasets: cdm.example_datasets()
path = cdm.eunomia_dir("GiBleed", cdm_version="5.3")  # downloads if needed
con = ibis.duckdb.connect(path)

cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(
    con,
    cdm_schema="main",
    write_schema="main",
    cdm_name="My CDM",
)
  • cdm_schema: Schema (database in Ibis terms) where CDM tables live. Required.
  • write_schema: Schema where you can create tables (e.g. cohorts). Required.
  • cdm_name: Optional; used to identify the CDM in output files. If omitted, inferred from cdm_source when present.

Access tables by name: cdm_ref.person, cdm_ref["observation_period"]. All table references are Ibis expressions; data stays in the database until you execute a query or call .to_pyarrow() / .to_pandas().

docs.title: In-memory CDM from tables
docs.text:
Build a CDM from a dict of table names to Arrow tables or pandas DataFrames:

import cdmconnector as cdm
import pyarrow as pa

person = pa.table({
    "person_id": [1, 2],
    "gender_concept_id": [0, 0],
    "year_of_birth": [1990, 1985],
    "race_concept_id": [0, 0],
    "ethnicity_concept_id": [0, 0],
})
observation_period = pa.table({
    "observation_period_id": [1, 2],
    "person_id": [1, 2],
    "observation_period_start_date": [...],  # dates
    "observation_period_end_date": [...],
    "period_type_concept_id": [0, 0],
})

cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_tables(
    {"person": person, "observation_period": observation_period},
    cdm_name="test",
    cdm_version="5.3",
)

This creates an in-memory DuckDB, registers the tables, and returns a Cdm with Ibis table references.

docs.title: Access tables and run queries
docs.text:
• Attribute / item access: cdm_ref.person, cdm_ref["person"].
  • Table list: cdm_ref.tables.
  • Metadata: cdm_ref.name, cdm_ref.version, cdm_ref.write_schema.

Use Ibis methods to build queries. The data stays in the database; SQL is generated and sent to the backend. Prefer aggregating in the database before bringing results into Python.

# Filter and select
q = cdm_ref.person.filter(cdm_ref.person.year_of_birth >= 1990).select("person_id", "year_of_birth")
q.execute()  # or .to_pyarrow() / .to_pandas()

docs.title: Joining tables
docs.text:
The OMOP CDM is relational; events use integer concept IDs. To get text descriptions, join clinical tables to the concept vocabulary table. Every OMOP CDM should include the vocabulary used for mapping.

Example: most common conditions in the CDM:

from ibis import _

# Count conditions, then join to concept for names
counts = (
    cdm_ref.condition_occurrence
    .group_by("condition_concept_id")
    .agg(n=_.count())
    .order_by(_.n.desc())
)
result = counts.join(
    cdm_ref.concept,
    counts.condition_concept_id == cdm_ref.concept.concept_id,
).select("condition_concept_id", "concept_name", "n")
result.to_pandas()

Example: most common drugs among patients with "Acute viral pharyngitis" (concept_id 4112343):

persons_with_condition = (
    cdm_ref.condition_occurrence
    .filter(_.condition_concept_id == 4112343)
    .select("person_id")
    .distinct()
)
drug_counts = (
    cdm_ref.drug_exposure
    .join(persons_with_condition, "person_id")
    .group_by("drug_concept_id")
    .agg(n=_.count())
    .order_by(_.n.desc())
)
result = drug_counts.join(
    cdm_ref.concept,
    drug_counts.drug_concept_id == cdm_ref.concept.concept_id,
).select("concept_name", "n")
result.to_pandas()

To inspect the generated SQL, use Ibis’s compile() or the backend’s equivalent. For more complex analyses (e.g. cohort definitions), see the Cohorts tutorial.

docs.title: Saving query results to the database
docs.text:
To persist a query result as a table in the write schema instead of loading it into memory, use compute:

# Ensure a write schema exists (e.g. "scratch"); then create CDM with write_schema="scratch"
drug_counts = (
    cdm_ref.condition_occurrence
    .filter(_.condition_concept_id == 4112343)
    .select("person_id")
    .distinct()
    .join(cdm_ref.drug_exposure, "person_id")
    .group_by("drug_concept_id")
    .agg(n=_.count())
    .order_by(_.n.desc())
)
drugs_with_names = drug_counts.join(
    cdm_ref.concept, drug_counts.drug_concept_id == cdm_ref.concept.concept_id
)
drugs_table = cdm.compute(drugs_with_names, name="test", temporary=False, overwrite=True)
# drugs_table is an Ibis reference to the new table in the write schema

docs.title: Selecting a subset of CDM tables
docs.text:
If you only need certain tables, create a slimmer CDM with cdm_select:

# By name
cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_select(cdm_ref, "person", "observation_period")

# Or keep only tables whose names start with "concept"
cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_select(cdm_ref, *[t for t in cdm_ref.tables if t.startswith("concept")])

docs.title: Eunomia example data
docs.text:
To use the Eunomia example datasets (e.g. GiBleed):

  1. Set the EUNOMIA_DATA_FOLDER environment variable to a directory path (optional; a temp directory is used if unset).
  2. Call cdm.eunomia_dir("GiBleed") to download and get the database path.
  3. Connect to that path with Ibis:
path = cdm.eunomia_dir("GiBleed", cdm_version="5.3")
con = ibis.duckdb.connect(path)
cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(con, cdm_schema="main", write_schema="main")

docs.title: Disconnect
docs.text:
For database-backed CDMs, close the connection when finished. After closing, any CDM objects created from that connection can no longer be used.

cdm.cdm_disconnect(cdm_ref)
# or
cdm_ref.disconnect()

Optional: cdm_ref.disconnect(drop_write_schema=True) to drop tables in the write schema before disconnecting.

docs.title: Ibis vs dplyr/dbplyr
docs.text: In R, CDMConnector uses dplyr and dbplyr for lazy queries and SQL pushdown. In Python, CDMConnector uses Ibis for the same role.

docs.title: Verb mapping
docs.text: R (dplyr/dbplyr) Python (Ibis) filter(...) .filter(...) mutate(...) .mutate(...) select(...) .select(...) group_by(...) %>% summarise(...) .group_by(...).aggregate(...) arrange(...) .order_by(...) collect() .execute() or .to_pyarrow() / .to_pandas() compute(name = ...) Backend create_table / materialize (see below)

docs.title: Materializing (compute)
docs.text:
In R, dplyr::compute(name = "tbl", temporary = FALSE) materializes a lazy query into a table. In Python with Ibis:

  • Use the backend’s create_table to materialize an expression into a table, or
  • Use .to_pyarrow() (or .execute()) and then write the result back with insert_table(cdm, name, result, overwrite=True).

A future release may add a compute(cdm, expr, name=..., temporary=...) helper that does this in one step.

docs.title: Using CDM attributes
docs.text: The CDM reference object and cohort tables carry metadata attributes that are useful for programming and for building analytic packages on top of CDMConnector. This tutorial describes how to access and use them.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Connect to an example CDM (e.g. Eunomia):

import cdmconnector as cdm
import ibis

path = cdm.eunomia_dir("GiBleed", cdm_version="5.3")
con = ibis.duckdb.connect(path)

cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(
    con,
    cdm_name="eunomia",
    cdm_schema="main",
    write_schema="main",
    cdm_version="5.3",
)

docs.title: CDM name
docs.text:
Every CDM reference has a name (e.g. for network studies, to associate results with a data source). Access it with cdm_name:

cdm.cdm_name(cdm_ref)
# or
cdm_ref.name

docs.title: CDM version
docs.text:
The OMOP CDM has multiple versions. The CDM reference stores the version of the connected CDM:

cdm.cdm_version(cdm_ref)
# or
cdm_ref.version

docs.title: Database connection
docs.text:
The underlying Ibis connection is available for low-level use (e.g. listing tables, running raw SQL):

con = cdm.cdm_con(cdm_ref)
# or
con = cdm_ref.source.con  # if your Cdm exposes the source

# Example: list tables in the connection (backend-dependent)
# con.list_tables() or equivalent

You can use this connection with Ibis or the backend’s native API to list tables, list columns, or run ad-hoc queries.

docs.title: Cohort attributes
docs.text:
When you generate a cohort (or add a cohort table with metadata), the cohort has associated attributes:

  • Cohort set – Defines cohort_definition_id and cohort_name. Access via the cohort’s cohort_set attribute or settings(cohort) if your package provides it.
  • Cohort count – Person and record counts per cohort. Use cdm.cohort_count(cohort).
  • Attrition – Step-by-step attrition. Use cdm.attrition(cohort).

docs.title: Custom cohort with metadata
docs.text:
If you build a custom cohort table (e.g. from a condition_occurrence filter), you can attach the same metadata so it behaves like a generated cohort set. Create a table with the four required columns (cohort_definition_id, subject_id, cohort_start_date, cohort_end_date), persist it with compute, then ensure the cohort set and attrition tables are created and linked.

Example: custom GI bleed cohort from condition_occurrence:

from ibis import _

# Build table with required cohort columns
gi_bleed = (
    cdm_ref.condition_occurrence
    .filter(_.condition_concept_id == 192671)
    .select(
        cohort_definition_id=_.literal(1),
        subject_id=_.person_id,
        cohort_start_date=_.condition_start_date,
        cohort_end_date=_.condition_start_date,
    )
)

# Persist to write schema
cdm_ref["gi_bleed"] = cdm.compute(gi_bleed, name="gi_bleed", temporary=False, overwrite=True)

Then add cohort set and attrition metadata (e.g. via your package’s new_cohort_table or by populating gi_bleed_set and gi_bleed_attrition). After that, cohort_count and attrition will work the same as for generated cohorts.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Use cdm_name and cdm_version to identify the CDM and its version.
  • Use cdm_con when you need the underlying connection for DBI-style operations.
  • Cohort tables can carry cohort_set, cohort_attrition, and counts; use cohort_count and attrition to read them, and attach metadata when creating custom cohorts so analytics behave consistently.

docs.title: CDM reference backends
docs.text: CDMConnector lets you work with CDM data in different locations in a consistent way. The CDM reference may point at tables in a database, or (after copying) in another database or schema. This tutorial shows how to copy a CDM from one backend to another so you can, for example, subset in the database and then work with the subset in a local DuckDB file.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
You can:

  1. Create a CDM reference from a database connection (cdm_from_con).
  2. Create a CDM reference from in-memory tables (cdm_from_tables).
  3. Copy an existing CDM into another connection (copy_cdm_to or insert_cdm_to).

All table references are Ibis expressions; you use the same Ibis API regardless of backend.

docs.title: Copying a CDM to another database
insert_cdm_to
docs.text:
insert_cdm_to(cdm, to_con, schema, overwrite=False) copies every table from the source CDM into the target Ibis connection to_con in the given schema. Each table is collected (executed) from the source and inserted into the target. It returns a new CDM reference pointing at the target.

Use this when you want to move a full CDM (or a subset you have already filtered) to another database (e.g. from Postgres to DuckDB for local analysis).

import cdmconnector as cdm
import ibis

# Source CDM (e.g. DuckDB Eunomia)
path_src = cdm.eunomia_dir("GiBleed")
con_src = ibis.duckdb.connect(path_src)
cdm_src = cdm.cdm_from_con(con_src, cdm_schema="main", write_schema="main", cdm_name="eunomia")

# Target (e.g. a new DuckDB file or another backend)
con_target = ibis.duckdb.connect("local_copy.duckdb")

cdm_target = cdm.insert_cdm_to(cdm_src, con_target, schema="main", overwrite=True)
# cdm_target now points at the tables in local_copy.duckdb

docs.title: copy_cdm_to
docs.text: copy_cdm_to(con, cdm, schema, overwrite=False) does the same conceptually but with argument order (target_connection, source_cdm, schema). It inserts person and observation_period first, then the rest of the tables, and returns a new CDM reference for the target. Use whichever signature fits your code (e.g. copy_cdm_to(con, cdm_ref, "main")).

docs.title: Example workflow: subset in DB, then copy to file
docs.text:
A typical pattern (as in the R “CDM reference backends” vignette) is:

  1. Connect to the source CDM (e.g. Postgres).
  2. Filter tables (e.g. restrict condition_occurrence to one concept_id, join to person).
  3. Copy the (possibly filtered) CDM to a local DuckDB file with insert_cdm_to or copy_cdm_to.
  4. Run further analysis or visualisation on the local copy.

Filtering and copying keeps the heavy work in the database and gives you a portable subset for downstream steps.

docs.title: Disconnect
docs.text:
After copying, disconnect the source CDM if you no longer need it:

cdm.cdm_disconnect(cdm_src)

docs.title: The CDM reference
docs.text:
A CDM reference is a single object that represents OMOP CDM data. The tables in the reference may live in a database (via Ibis), or in memory (Arrow/pandas). CDMConnector provides a general notion of a CDM reference and implementations for database-backed and in-memory sources.

This tutorial describes the structure and rules of a CDM reference, as defined in the R omopgenerics package and mirrored in CDMConnector (Python).

docs.title: What is in a CDM reference?
docs.text: A CDM reference is a collection of tables. These fall into three categories:

docs.title: 1. Standard OMOP CDM tables
docs.text:
The OMOP CDM has multiple versions. The list of standard tables for a given version (e.g. 5.3) can be obtained with:

import cdmconnector as cdm

cdm.omop_tables(version="5.3")

Standard OMOP tables have required columns. You can check them with:

cdm.omop_columns(table="person", version="5.3")
cdm.omop_columns(table="observation_period", version="5.3")

docs.title: 2. Cohort tables
docs.text:
Studies often create study-specific cohort tables. Once created, these are part of the CDM reference. Each cohort table is associated with metadata: cohort set (names/IDs) and cohort attrition. Cohort tables are expected to have a specific set of columns:

cdm.cohort_columns(table="cohort", version="5.3")
cdm.cohort_columns(table="cohort_set", version="5.3")
cdm.cohort_columns(table="cohort_attrition", version="5.3")

docs.title: 3. Other tables
docs.text: Beyond standard OMOP and cohort tables, you can add other tables (e.g. OMOP extensions or study-specific tables). They can have any columns; only naming conventions (lower snake_case) need to be respected if you want consistency with the ecosystem.

docs.title: Rules for a CDM reference
docs.text:
For an object to be a valid CDM reference:

  • Single source – All tables must share the same source (e.g. all in one database or all in memory; you cannot mix database and in-memory tables in one reference).
  • Table names – Lower snake_case.
  • Column names – Lower snake_case for every table.
  • Required tables – The person and observation_period tables must be present.
  • cdm_name – The reference must have a name (e.g. for multi-database studies).

docs.title: Exporting CDM metadata (snapshot)
docs.text:
When you export metadata about a CDM (e.g. for sharing or documentation), a snapshot typically includes:

Variable Description result_type e.g. "Snapshot" cdm_name Name of the data source cdm_version CDM version (e.g. 5.3) person_count Number of rows in person observation_period_count Number of rows in observation_period earliest_observation_period_start_date Min observation_period_start_date latest_observation_period_end_date Max observation_period_end_date snapshot_date When the snapshot was created

Optional fields (e.g. from cdm_source) can include: cdm_source_name, cdm_description, vocabulary_version, etc.

In CDMConnector you can use snapshot(cdm) to produce a summary of the CDM for export or reporting.

docs.title: Concept sets and codelists
docs.text:
In the OMOP ecosystem, concept sets are used to define groups of OMOP concepts (e.g. for cohort definitions or analyses). They can be represented in two main ways:

  1. Codelist – A named list of concept IDs (no hierarchy or exclusions).
  2. Concept set expression – A higher-level definition that, when applied to a specific OMOP vocabulary (using concept hierarchies and relationships), expands to a set of concept IDs.

This tutorial summarizes the omopgenerics (R) approach. The Python CDMConnector may expose helpers for codelists/concept sets in the future; the structure below is the standard used in the OHDSI/DARWIN ecosystem.

docs.title: Codelist
docs.text:
A codelist is a named list where each element is a set of concept IDs.

  • Must be named – Each set has a name (e.g. "diabetes", "asthma").
  • No missing values – Concept IDs cannot be NA/None.

Example (R-style structure; in Python you might use a dict):

# Conceptual: named list of concept ID lists
condition_codes = {
    "diabetes": [201820, 4087682, 3655269],
    "asthma": [317009],
}

Validation rules in omopgenerics: names required, no NA in concept IDs.

docs.title: Concept set expression
docs.text:
A concept set expression defines concepts at a higher level, with options for:

  • descendants – Include descendant concepts in the vocabulary hierarchy.
  • excluded – Exclude specific concepts.
  • mapped – Include mapped (non-standard) concepts.

It is typically a table per concept set with columns such as: concept_id, excluded, descendants, mapped. When applied to a given OMOP vocabulary version, this expands to an actual list of concept IDs.

Example (R-style tibble; in Python you might use a DataFrame or list of dicts):

# Conceptual: per-set table with concept_id, excluded, descendants, mapped
condition_cs = {
    "diabetes": [
        {"concept_id": 201820, "excluded": False, "descendants": True, "mapped": False},
        {"concept_id": 4087682, "excluded": False, "descendants": False, "mapped": False},
    ],
    "asthma": [
        {"concept_id": 317009, "excluded": False, "descendants": False, "mapped": False},
    ],
}

As with codelists, concept set expressions must be named and cannot contain missing values in required fields.

docs.title: Use in CDMConnector
docs.text: Cohort generation (e.g. generate_concept_cohort_set) accepts concept sets as named lists of concept IDs (codelist-style). Concept set expressions are typically expanded to codelists in R (e.g. using Capr or vocabulary tables) before being passed to cohort builders. In Python, you can pass a dict of list of concept IDs to generate_concept_cohort_set; support for concept set expressions may be added in a future release.

docs.title: Cohort tables (omopgenerics)
docs.text: In omopgenerics (R) and the OHDSI ecosystem, a cohort table is more than a raw table: it is an object that includes the cohort rows plus metadata (cohort set, attrition, and optionally codelists). This tutorial describes that structure and how it aligns with CDMConnector (Python).

docs.title: Cohort table object
docs.text:
A cohort table is defined by:

  1. Cohort table – The actual rows: cohort_definition_id, subject_id, cohort_start_date, cohort_end_date (and optionally more columns).
  2. Cohort set – Maps cohort_definition_id to cohort_name and other definition metadata.
  3. Cohort attrition – Step-by-step attrition (reason_id, reason, number_subjects, number_records, excluded_subjects, excluded_records).

In Python CDMConnector:

  • When you use generate_cohort_set or generate_concept_cohort_set, the package creates the cohort table and the associated *_set and *_attrition tables.
  • You access counts with cohort_count(cohort) and full attrition with attrition(cohort). The cohort object is typically the Ibis table reference plus attached metadata (e.g. cohort_set, cohort_attrition attributes).

docs.title: Creating a cohort table from existing data
docs.text:
If you have a CDM reference with just person and observation_period, you can add a cohort by:

  1. Building a table with the four required columns.
  2. Inserting it into the CDM (e.g. with insert_table or compute).
  3. Ensuring cohort set and attrition tables exist and are linked.

Example (conceptual): two people, one has an event of interest; add a cohort row for that person:

import cdmconnector as cdm
import pyarrow as pa

# Minimal CDM
person = pa.table({
    "person_id": [1, 2],
    "gender_concept_id": [0, 0],
    "year_of_birth": [1990, 1990],
    "race_concept_id": [0, 0],
    "ethnicity_concept_id": [0, 0],
})
observation_period = pa.table({
    "observation_period_id": [1, 2],
    "person_id": [1, 2],
    "observation_period_start_date": [pa.scalar("2000-01-01")] * 2,
    "observation_period_end_date": [pa.scalar("2021-12-31")] * 2,
    "period_type_concept_id": [0, 0],
})

cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_tables(
    {"person": person, "observation_period": observation_period},
    cdm_name="example_cdm",
    cdm_version="5.3",
)

# Cohort: one row for person 1
cohort = pa.table({
    "cohort_definition_id": [1],
    "subject_id": [1],
    "cohort_start_date": [pa.scalar("2020-01-01")],
    "cohort_end_date": [pa.scalar("2020-01-10")],
})
# Insert and attach set/attrition (implementation depends on your backend)
# cdm_ref = cdm.insert_table(cdm_ref, "cohort", cohort, overwrite=True)
# Then attach cohort_set and cohort_attrition so cohort_count() and attrition() work.

docs.title: Cohort set requirements
docs.text:
• Lower-case column names.
  • At least: cohort_definition_id, cohort_name.
  • Unique cohort names; every ID in the cohort table must appear in the set.

docs.title: Cohort attrition requirements
docs.text:
• Lower-case column names.
  • At least: cohort_definition_id, reason_id, reason, number_subjects, number_records, and optionally excluded_subjects, excluded_records.
  • Unique pairs of (cohort_definition_id, reason_id).
  • All cohort_definition_ids in the cohort table must appear in attrition.

docs.title: Cohort table requirements
docs.text:
• Same source as the CDM reference.
  • Lower snake_case column names.
  • Required columns: cohort_definition_id, subject_id, cohort_start_date, cohort_end_date.
  • No NA in required columns.
  • cohort_start_date ≤ cohort_end_date.
  • No overlapping periods for the same person within the same cohort_definition_id.
  • All person-days between start and end must fall within an observation period.

docs.title: Combining cohort tables (bind)
docs.text:
You can combine multiple cohort tables into one with bind, as long as cohort names are unique across the combined set. In Python:

combined = cdm.bind(cdm_ref["cohort_a"], cdm_ref["cohort_b"], name="combined_cohorts")

docs.title: Exporting cohort metadata
docs.text: Cohort metadata (set, counts, attrition) can be exported for sharing or documentation. In R omopgenerics this is done via summary(cohort) producing a summarised_result. In Python, you can use cohort_count(cohort) and attrition(cohort) and export those tables or include them in a snapshot.

docs.title: CohortCharacteristics: Large-scale characteristics
docs.text: This tutorial summarises the R CohortCharacteristics vignette on large-scale characterisation: summarising all (or many) concepts that appear in clinical tables in a window around cohort entry, rather than pre-specified characteristics only.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: In Summarise characteristics we summarise a fixed set of variables (age, sex, prior observation, specific cohort/concept/table intersects). Large-scale characterisation instead asks: “For everyone in my cohort, what conditions, procedures, and drugs did they have in a given window (e.g. any time before index, or on index date)?” and returns summaries for every concept that appears above a frequency threshold (e.g. ≥10% of the cohort).

docs.title: summariseLargeScaleCharacteristics()
docs.text:
• window – List of windows (e.g. list(c(-Inf, -1), c(0, 0)) = prior to index, on index date).
  • eventInWindow – Tables where a single date defines the event (e.g. condition_occurrence, procedure_occurrence): “record started in window”.
  • episodeInWindow – Tables where an interval matters (e.g. drug_exposure): “record overlapped window” (new or ongoing exposure).
  • minimumFrequency – Only return concepts for which at least this fraction of the cohort has a record (e.g. 0.1 = 10%).

Output is a summarised_result with one row per (cohort, window, concept, variable_level, estimate). Often there are many concepts, so the package provides:

  • tableLargeScaleCharacteristics(lsc) – Full table.
  • tableTopLargeScaleCharacteristics(lsc, topConcepts = 5) – Top N concepts by frequency.

docs.title: Stratified large-scale characteristics
docs.text: You can add strata (e.g. sex) to the cohort table and pass strata to summariseLargeScaleCharacteristics() so that large-scale summaries are produced per stratum. Same table/plot helpers then show results by stratum (e.g. facet by sex).

docs.title: In Python
docs.text:
No CohortCharacteristics port yet. You can:

  1. For each cohort and window, join the cohort table to condition_occurrence, procedure_occurrence, drug_exposure (filtering by window: event date in window, or interval overlapping window).
  2. Group by concept_id (and optionally cohort, window, stratum), count distinct persons and records, compute percentage.
  3. Filter to concepts with percentage ≥ minimumFrequency.
  4. Shape as summarised_result (variable_name = “condition”/“procedure”/“drug”, variable_level = concept_id or concept_name, estimates = count, percentage).
  5. Optionally sort by frequency and take top N for a “top concepts” table.

Implementing this in Ibis keeps the work in the database and produces the same structure as R for downstream tables and plots.

docs.title: CohortCharacteristics: Cohort overlap and timing
docs.text: This tutorial summarises the R CohortCharacteristics vignettes on cohort overlap (how many individuals appear in multiple cohorts) and cohort timing (time between entering different cohorts).

docs.title: Cohort overlap
docs.text: When you have multiple cohorts (e.g. medication cohorts: acetaminophen, morphine, warfarin), you often want to know overlap: how many people are in more than one cohort, and in which combinations.

docs.title: summariseCohortOverlap()
docs.text:
• Input: A cohort table with several cohort_definition_ids (e.g. one per drug).
  • Output: A summarised_result with overlap counts: for each pair (or set) of cohorts, how many individuals appear in both (or all). You can include or exclude “unique” combinations (e.g. only A∩B vs also B∩A) with uniqueCombinations.

docs.title: Tables and plots
docs.text:
• tableCohortOverlap(medsOverlap, uniqueCombinations = FALSE) – Formatted table of overlap counts.
  • plotCohortOverlap(medsOverlap, ...) – Visualisation of overlap (e.g. Venn-style or heatmap).

docs.title: Stratified overlap
docs.text: Add strata (e.g. age_group) to the cohort table, then call summariseCohortOverlap(strata = list("age_group")). Table and plot functions accept facet (e.g. by age_group) to show overlap within each stratum.

docs.title: Cohort timing
docs.text: Cohort timing is the time between entering one cohort and another (e.g. time from first acetaminophen to first morphine). Useful for drug sequencing, event order, etc.

docs.title: summariseCohortTiming()
docs.text:
• Input: A cohort table with multiple cohort_definition_ids (e.g. one per drug).
  • Output: A summarised_result with timing statistics (e.g. median, q25, q75, min, max days/years between first entry in cohort A and first entry in cohort B).
  • restrictToFirstEntry: If TRUE, only the first record per person per cohort is used, so you get “time from first exposure to A to first exposure to B”.

docs.title: Tables and plots
docs.text:
• tableCohortTiming(medsTiming, timeScale = "years", uniqueCombinations = FALSE) – Table of timing by cohort pair.
  • plotCohortTiming(..., plotType = "boxplot", timeScale = "years") – Boxplot of time between cohorts.
  • plotCohortTiming(..., plotType = "densityplot", ...) – Density of timing; requires density in the estimates when calling summariseCohortTiming(density = TRUE).

docs.title: Stratified timing
docs.text: Add strata (e.g. age_group) to the cohort table, then summariseCohortTiming(strata = list("age_group"), density = TRUE). Use facet and colour in plotCohortTiming() to stratify.

docs.title: In Python
docs.text:
No CohortCharacteristics port yet. You can:

  1. Overlap: From the cohort table, for each pair of cohort_definition_ids, count distinct subject_ids that appear in both (e.g. with Ibis: join cohort to itself on subject_id, filter to different cohort_definition_id, count). Shape as summarised_result (group/strata = cohort pair, variable = “overlap”, estimate = count).
  2. Timing: For each person and each ordered pair of cohorts, get first cohort_start_date for each cohort, compute difference in days/years, then aggregate (median, q25, q75, etc.) per cohort pair. Again, output in summarised_result format so the same table/plot conventions apply.

Keeping the same result layout allows reuse of R’s table/plot functions or a future Python equivalent (e.g. visOmopResults-style).

docs.title: CohortCharacteristics: Summarise patient characteristics
docs.text: This tutorial summarises the R CohortCharacteristics vignette on summarising the characteristics of individuals in study cohorts (demographics, prior/future observation, cohort overlaps, concept/table intersections) and displaying them as tables and plots in standardised form.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
CohortCharacteristics builds on PatientProfiles and summarised_result. You start with a CDM and one or more cohorts (e.g. injury cohorts: ankle sprain, ankle fracture, forearm fracture, hip fracture). You then:

  1. Summarise characteristics (age, sex, prior/future observation, cohort/concept/table intersections) into a summarised_result.
  2. Display results with tableCharacteristics() (e.g. gt table) and plotCharacteristics() (e.g. boxplot, barplot).

The workflow uses CDMConnector (or equivalent) to create cohorts, PatientProfiles-style logic to add demographics and intersections, and a summarise step that produces the standard summarised_result format.

docs.title: Basic workflow
docs.text:
1. Create study cohorts – e.g. generateConceptCohortSet() with concept sets for each injury (or use Atlas cohorts).
  2. Summarise characteristics – Call summariseCharacteristics() on the cohort table. You can pass:
  3. ageGroup – List of age ranges (e.g. list(c(0, 49), c(50, Inf))).
  4. strata – Variables to stratify by (e.g. list("age_group") after adding age group to the cohort).
  5. cohortIntersectFlag – Named list of “label = list(targetCohortTable, window)” to add flags like “Medications prior to index date”.
  6. conceptIntersectFlag – Same idea with concept sets instead of cohort tables.

  7. tableIntersectCount / tableIntersectFlag – Counts or flags from OMOP tables (e.g. visit_occurrence, drug_exposure) in a window.

  8. Table – tableCharacteristics(chars) produces a formatted table (e.g. gt) with number of subjects/records, age, dates, prior/future observation, days in cohort, and any intersect variables.

  9. Plot – Filter to one variable (e.g. variable_name == "Age"), then plotCharacteristics(..., plotType = "boxplot", colour = "cohort_name", facet = c("cdm_name")).

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text:
• Days in cohort: cohort_end_date − cohort_start_date + 1 (duration in the cohort).
  • Future observation: time from cohort_start_date to observation_period_end_date.
  • Prior observation: time from observation_period_start_date to cohort_start_date.

docs.title: Cohort vs concept vs table intersects
docs.text:
• cohortIntersectFlag: Uses another cohort table; counts/flags cohort entries in the window (after cohort rules, e.g. collapse overlaps, observation period).
  • conceptIntersectFlag: Uses concept sets; works on record-level data, so counts can differ from cohort-based.
  • tableIntersectCount/Flag: Uses any OMOP table (or subset) in a window; flexible for visits, procedures, drug exposures, etc.

docs.title: Stratified summaries
docs.text: Add variables (e.g. age_group, sex) to the cohort table (e.g. with PatientProfiles’ addAge(), addSex()), then pass strata to summariseCharacteristics(). Use groupColumn in tableCharacteristics() to group rows (e.g. by age_group). Use facet and colour in plotCharacteristics() to stratify plots.

docs.title: In Python
docs.text:
There is no CohortCharacteristics port yet. You can:

  1. Use CDMConnector to build cohorts and Ibis (or PatientProfiles-style logic) to add demographics and intersections.
  2. Aggregate into the summarised_result layout (see Summarised result).
  3. Build tables and plots with pandas/matplotlib/plotly or export to R for gt/ggplot2. Keeping the same column names and estimate structure allows reuse of R’s table/plot helpers or a future Python visOmopResults-style package.

docs.title: Database connection examples
docs.text:
CDMConnector uses Ibis for all table and query operations. You create an Ibis connection to your database, then pass it to cdm_from_con with the appropriate schema arguments. The following examples show how to connect from Python for common backends.

Environment variables

Store credentials in environment variables (e.g. CDM5_POSTGRESQL_HOST, CDM5_POSTGRESQL_USER) or a secrets manager instead of hardcoding them.

docs.title: DuckDB
docs.text:
DuckDB is a file-based, in-process database similar to SQLite, with strong date and analytical support. It is ideal for local or example CDM data.

import cdmconnector as cdm
import ibis

# From a file path (e.g. Eunomia)
path = cdm.eunomia_dir("GiBleed", cdm_version="5.3")
con = ibis.duckdb.connect(path)

cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(
    con,
    cdm_schema="main",
    write_schema="main",
    cdm_name="eunomia",
)

# When finished
cdm.cdm_disconnect(cdm_ref)

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text:
Connect to PostgreSQL using Ibis’s Postgres backend:

import ibis
import os

con = ibis.postgres.connect(
    host=os.environ.get("CDM5_POSTGRESQL_HOST", "localhost"),
    port=5432,
    user=os.environ.get("CDM5_POSTGRESQL_USER"),
    password=os.environ.get("CDM5_POSTGRESQL_PASSWORD"),
    database=os.environ.get("CDM5_POSTGRESQL_DBNAME", "cdm"),
)

cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(
    con,
    cdm_schema=os.environ.get("CDM5_POSTGRESQL_CDM_SCHEMA", "cdm"),
    write_schema=os.environ.get("CDM5_POSTGRESQL_SCRATCH_SCHEMA", "scratch"),
    cdm_name="my_cdm",
)

Install the Postgres backend if needed: pip install ibis-framework[postgres] (or the package name per Ibis docs).

docs.title: Snowflake
docs.text:
Connect to Snowflake using Ibis’s Snowflake backend:

import ibis
import os

con = ibis.snowflake.connect(
    account=os.environ.get("SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT"),
    user=os.environ.get("SNOWFLAKE_USER"),
    password=os.environ.get("SNOWFLAKE_PASSWORD"),
    database=os.environ.get("SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE"),
    warehouse=os.environ.get("SNOWFLAKE_WAREHOUSE"),
    schema=os.environ.get("SNOWFLAKE_SCHEMA", "CDM53"),
)

# For CDMConnector, cdm_schema and write_schema may be catalog/schema dicts
# depending on how your CDM is organized (see db-backends.md).
cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(
    con,
    cdm_schema={"database": "OMOP_DB", "schema": "CDM53"},
    write_schema={"database": "OMOP_DB", "schema": "RESULTS"},
    cdm_name="my_cdm",
)

Install: pip install ibis-framework[snowflake] (or per Ibis docs).

docs.title: Google BigQuery
docs.text:
import ibis
import os

con = ibis.bigquery.connect(
    project_id=os.environ.get("BIGQUERY_PROJECT"),
    dataset_id=os.environ.get("BIGQUERY_CDM_DATASET", "cdm"),
)

cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(
    con,
    cdm_schema=os.environ.get("BIGQUERY_CDM_DATASET", "cdm"),
    write_schema=os.environ.get("BIGQUERY_SCRATCH_DATASET", "scratch"),
    cdm_name="my_cdm",
)

Install: pip install ibis-framework[bigquery].

docs.title: SQL Server
docs.text:
Ibis can connect to SQL Server via ODBC or other drivers. Schema is often expressed as database.schema (e.g. cdmv54.dbo). Check the Ibis SQL Server documentation for the exact connection API and required packages.

import ibis
import os

# Example pattern (actual API may vary by Ibis version)
# con = ibis.mssql.connect(...)

# cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(
#     con,
#     cdm_schema=("cdmv54", "dbo"),
#     write_schema=("tempdb", "dbo"),
#     cdm_name="my_cdm",
# )

docs.title: Databricks / Spark
docs.text:
For Spark/Databricks, use Ibis’s Spark backend. Connection details (host, token, http_path, etc.) are typically set via environment variables or Databricks configuration.

import ibis
import os

# Example pattern; see Ibis Spark/Databricks docs for exact parameters
# con = ibis.spark.connect(...)

# cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(
#     con,
#     cdm_schema="gibleed",
#     write_schema="scratch",
#     cdm_name="my_cdm",
# )

docs.title: Schema handling
docs.text:
• cdm_schema: Schema (or catalog + schema) where OMOP CDM tables live. Required.
  • write_schema: Schema where you can create tables (e.g. cohorts). Required.
  • For backends that use catalog + schema (e.g. Snowflake), pass a dict: {"catalog": "...", "schema": "..."}.

See Database backends for more on schema handling and optional features (e.g. write_prefix).

docs.title: Expanding the CDMConnector/omopgenerics ecosystem
docs.text: The R omopgenerics package defines an ecosystem of methods and classes, including the cdm_source abstraction. Different “sources” (e.g. local in-memory, database via DBI) implement the same interface so that CDM references can be created and used in a uniform way. This tutorial summarises how new sources are added in R so that Python implementations can follow the same pattern.

docs.title: Source object
docs.text: A source is an object (e.g. a list or class instance) that represents where CDM tables live (database, files, in-memory). It has a type (e.g. "local_cdm", "db_cdm") and implements a set of methods. In R, the source is validated with omopgenerics::newCdmSource(src, sourceType = "my_type").

docs.title: Required methods
docs.text:
To define a new source (e.g. a new database backend or file-based store), you implement:

  1. insertTable(cdm, name, table, overwrite, temporary) – Insert a local table into the source. Returns a reference that can be wrapped as a cdm_table.
  2. compute(x, name, temporary, overwrite) – Materialise a query x as a table in the source. Returns a reference to the new table.
  3. listSourceTables(cdm) – List table names in the source.
  4. dropSourceTable(cdm, name) – Drop a table from the source.

docs.title: Recommended methods
docs.text:
1. readSourceTable(cdm, name) – Read a table from the source as a cdm_table-like reference.
  2. insertCdmTo(cdm, to) – Copy a full CDM reference into this source (e.g. for moving data from one backend to another).
  3. summary(object) – Return metadata about the source (e.g. connection details, type) as a named list of strings.

docs.title: Creating a CDM reference from a custom source
docs.text:
A function that builds a CDM reference from your source (e.g. cdmFromMyCustomSource(...)) should:

  1. Use the source’s readSourceTable (or equivalent) to get references for each OMOP table (and any Achilles/cohort tables).
  2. Call the package’s “new CDM reference” constructor with: tables (list of table references), cdmName, cdmVersion.
  3. Optionally attach cohort tables and their set/attrition metadata.

All table references must share the same source. Table and column names should be lower snake_case; person and observation_period must be present.

docs.title: In Python CDMConnector
docs.text: CDMConnector already has DbCdmSource (database via Ibis) and LocalCdmSource (in-memory). To add a new backend (e.g. Arrow files, another SQL engine), implement the same operations: insert table, compute query, list tables, drop table, and optionally read table and insert CDM. Then add a cdm_from_* function that builds a Cdm from that source using the same schema and naming rules. This keeps behaviour consistent with R and with other Python code that expects a standard CDM reference.

docs.title: Ibis queries with CDMConnector
docs.text: This tutorial shows how to use Ibis to query OMOP CDM tables created with CDMConnector. All CDM table references are Ibis expressions; you build queries with Ibis methods and execute them when you need results.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Load CDMConnector and connect to an example CDM (e.g. Eunomia):

import cdmconnector as cdm
import ibis

path = cdm.eunomia_dir("GiBleed", cdm_version="5.3")
con = ibis.duckdb.connect(path)
cdm_ref = cdm.cdm_from_con(con, cdm_name="eunomia", cdm_schema="main", write_schema="main")

docs.title: Histogram of year of birth
docs.text:
Select year_of_birth from the person table, bring it into memory, and plot:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = cdm_ref.person.select("year_of_birth").to_pandas()
df["year_of_birth"].hist(bins=30)
plt.xlabel("Year of birth")
plt.ylabel("Person count")
plt.title("Age distribution")
plt.show()

docs.title: Boxplot of observation period length
docs.text:
Compute observation period length in years on the database, then plot:

from ibis import _

obs = cdm_ref.observation_period
obs_years = obs.mutate(
    observation_period_years=(
        (obs.observation_period_end_date - obs.observation_period_start_date) / 365.25
    )
).select("observation_period_years")

df = obs_years.to_pandas()
df.boxplot(column="observation_period_years")
plt.ylabel("Observation period (years)")
plt.show()

docs.title: Behind the scenes: generated SQL
docs.text:
Ibis builds SQL from your expressions. To see the SQL that would be sent to the database, use compile() on the Ibis expression (or the backend’s equivalent).

Example: count rows in the person table:

q = cdm_ref.person.count()
# DuckDB backend: q.compile() or inspect the expression

Example: median year of birth:

from ibis import _

q = cdm_ref.person.agg(_.year_of_birth.median())
# Inspect q to see the generated SQL

Example: add a derived column (e.g. gender label) and inspect the query:

from ibis import _

q = cdm_ref.person.mutate(
    gender=_.gender_concept_id.case()
    .when(8507, "Male")
    .when(8532, "Female")
    .else_("Unknown")
    .end()
)
# q is an Ibis table expression; execute with q.to_pandas() or q.execute()

docs.title: Disconnect
docs.text:
When finished, close the connection:

cdm.cdm_disconnect(cdm_ref)

docs.title: Logging with omopgenerics
docs.text: Logging is commonly used in studies to record timings, steps, and errors, especially when sharing or reproducing code. The R omopgenerics package provides simple logging helpers that write to a file and can be exported as part of a summarised result. This tutorial describes that behaviour so you can mirror it in Python if needed.

docs.title: omopgenerics logging (R)
docs.text:
omopgenerics does not replace full-featured logging packages; it provides three functions:

  1. createLogFile(logFile) – Creates (or designates) the log file. Typically the path can include placeholders like {date} and {time}.
  2. logMessage(message) – Writes a message to the log file and echoes it to the console. If no log file has been created, the message is only printed to the console.
  3. summariseLogFile() – Reads the log file and formats it into a summarised_result object (e.g. for export with other results).

docs.title: Example workflow (R)
docs.text:
createLogFile(logFile = tempfile(pattern = "log_{date}_{time}"))

logMessage("Generating random numbers")
x <- runif(1e6)

logMessage("Calculating the sum")
result <- sum(x)

# Export log as summarised_result
log_sr <- summariseLogFile()

The log file path is typically stored in an option (e.g. options("omopgenerics.logFile" = path)).

docs.title: Export with summarised results
docs.text: When you export summarised results (e.g. exportSummarisedResult(result, path)), the log file can be exported as well. The imported bundle then includes a result type such as "summarise_log_file" so you can filter and view the log alongside other results.

docs.title: In Python
docs.text:
Python has a standard logging module and many third-party loggers. To align with omopgenerics:

  • Write log messages to a file and optionally to the console.
  • When exporting “summarised result”–style outputs, include a log artifact (e.g. a CSV or text file) and tag it in your metadata (e.g. result_type = "summarise_log_file") so it can be imported and displayed in the same way as in R.

You do not need to implement summariseLogFile() unless you want to represent the log as a summarised_result table; the main goal is consistent export/import and traceability.

docs.title: PatientProfiles: Cohort intersections
docs.text: This tutorial summarises the R PatientProfiles vignette on adding cohort intersection variables: whether and how often a person appears in another cohort within a time window, and the date or days to that intersection.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Cohorts are often used together: e.g. outcomes in an exposed cohort, or baseline comorbidities in a study population. Cohort intersections answer: “For each row in my cohort of interest (e.g. index date = cohort_start_date), did this person appear in another cohort table in a given window? How many times? When? How many days until that event?”

PatientProfiles provides four kinds of variables:

  1. addCohortIntersectFlag – Binary: did the person have at least one overlapping record in the target cohort in the window?
  2. addCohortIntersectCount – Count of overlapping records (or cohort entries) in the window.
  3. addCohortIntersectDate – Date of the intersection (e.g. target cohort_start_date); first or last in window.
  4. addCohortIntersectDays – Days from the index date to that intersection date.

docs.title: Index date and window
docs.text:
• Index date: Usually the cohort start (or end) date of the table you are enriching (e.g. cohort_start_date).
  • Window: e.g. [-180, 180] = from 180 days before to 180 days after the index date. You can use [-Inf, -1], [0, 0], [1, Inf] for “any time before”, “on index date”, “any time after”.
  • Target cohort: Another cohort table; for each target cohort_definition_id you get one (or more) new columns.

docs.title: Target cohort date range
docs.text:
For flag and count, you decide what “overlap” means:

  • targetStartDate and targetEndDate (e.g. cohort_start_date, cohort_end_date): an overlap exists if the target cohort’s [start, end] overlaps the window. So “ongoing” exposure in the window.
  • If you use the same date for start and end (e.g. cohort_start_date only), you are counting “events” (starts) that fall in the window.

So: same person, same window; one method counts records/entries in the window, the other can count starts only.

docs.title: First vs last intersection
docs.text: For date and days, you pick one date per target record (e.g. cohort_start_date). Then you choose order: first or last occurrence in the window. That gives one date and one “days to” value per row.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text: PatientProfiles supports a nameStyle with placeholders like {cohort_name} and {window_name} so you get columns such as prior_aspirin, future_morphine. If the target table has multiple cohorts, you get one variable per cohort (and per window if you pass multiple windows).

docs.title: In Python
docs.text:
With CDMConnector and Ibis you can:

  1. Join your cohort table (with index date) to the target cohort table on subject_id/person_id.
  2. Filter to rows where the target’s [start, end] (or target date) falls in your window relative to the index date.
  3. Aggregate: any → flag, count → count; for date/days take min or max date in the window and compute days from index date.

Implementing this in Ibis gives you the same semantics (flag, count, first/last date, days) and keeps execution in the database. Naming can follow the same {cohort_name}_{window_name} style for compatibility with downstream R/Python summarised_result consumers.

docs.title: PatientProfiles: Concept intersections
docs.text: This tutorial summarises the R PatientProfiles vignette on adding variables based on concept sets (e.g. drug ingredients) without first building a cohort. Results can differ from cohort-based intersections because concept-based logic works on record-level data.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Concept sets define groups of OMOP concepts (e.g. a list of concept IDs for “acetaminophen”). You can:

  1. Build a cohort from that concept set and then use cohort intersections, or
  2. Add concept-intersect variables directly from the clinical table (e.g. drug_exposure) filtered by concept ID.

PatientProfiles provides four concept-based functions, parallel to cohort intersections:

  • addConceptIntersectFlag – Had any record with that concept in the window?
  • addConceptIntersectCount – Number of records with that concept in the window.
  • addConceptIntersectDate – Date of first/last such record in the window.
  • addConceptIntersectDays – Days from index date to that record.

docs.title: Cohort vs concept intersections
docs.text:
• Cohort: Built from a concept set with rules (e.g. collapse overlapping spells, restrict to observation period). So addCohortIntersectCount counts cohort entries (after those rules).
  • Concept: Uses raw clinical table rows. addConceptIntersectCount counts records in the window.

So for the same concept set, record count can be higher than cohort entry count (e.g. overlapping drug exposures become one cohort entry but multiple records). Cohorts also often exclude records outside observation period; concept-based logic typically does not. Choose the one that matches your study definition.

docs.title: Multiple windows and multiple concept sets
docs.text: You can pass multiple windows (e.g. prior, index day, future) and get one variable per window. You can pass multiple concept sets (e.g. several drug ingredients) and get one set of columns per set (and optionally per window), with naming like {concept_set_name}_{window_name}.

docs.title: In Python
docs.text:
With CDMConnector and Ibis:

  1. Filter the clinical table (e.g. drug_exposure) by drug_concept_id in your concept list.
  2. Join to your cohort/table of interest on person_id and filter by your index date and window (record date or [start, end] overlapping window).
  3. Aggregate: any → flag, count → count; min/max date → first/last date; compute days from index date.

This gives you concept-based flag, count, date, and days in the same way as PatientProfiles, and you can align naming and output format with summarised_result for use in CohortCharacteristics or visOmopResults-style tables and plots.

docs.title: PatientProfiles: Adding patient demographics
docs.text: This tutorial summarises the R PatientProfiles vignette on adding patient-level demographics (age, sex, prior/future observation) to OMOP CDM tables and cohort tables. In Python you can implement the same logic with CDMConnector and Ibis.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
The OMOP CDM is person-centric. The person table identifies each individual and holds demographic fields: date of birth (often as year_of_birth, with month/day in extensions or imputed), gender, race, and ethnicity (as concept IDs). The observation_period table holds spans of time during which each person is under observation; periods cannot overlap.

When analysing events (e.g. condition_occurrence, drug_exposure) or cohorts, we often need characteristics at a date of interest: age at that date, sex, whether the person was under observation, and prior/future observation time.

docs.title: Adding characteristics to OMOP CDM tables
Age at an index date
docs.text:
Concept: Join the event table to person and compute age from date of birth to an index date (e.g. condition_start_date). PatientProfiles’ addAge(indexDate = "condition_start_date") does this; you can add age groups (e.g. 0–17, 18–65, ≥66) in the same step.

In Python (Ibis): Join condition_occurrence to person, then compute age and optional age groups:

from ibis import _

# Age in years at condition_start_date (assuming year_of_birth only)
t = cdm_ref.condition_occurrence.join(cdm_ref.person, "person_id")
t = t.mutate(
    age=_.condition_start_date.year() - t.year_of_birth
)
# Optional: age_group based on buckets

If you have full birth dates, use day difference divided by 365.25 or use Ibis date functions.

docs.title: Sex
docs.text:
Concept: Sex is time-invariant; add the gender concept (or a label like Male/Female) from the person table. PatientProfiles’ addSex() joins to person and maps gender_concept_id to a label.

In Python: Join to person and map 8507 → "Male", 8532 → "Female" (or use the concept table for labels).

docs.title: Prior and future observation
docs.text:
Concept: For each row (e.g. condition start date), determine the observation period that contains that date, then compute:

  • Prior observation: days from observation_period_start_date to the index date.
  • Future observation: days from the index date to observation_period_end_date.
  • In observation: whether the index date (or a window around it) falls inside an observation period.

PatientProfiles provides addPriorObservation(), addFutureObservation(), and addInObservation(); the latter can require a full window (e.g. 180 days before to 30 days after) or a minimum number of days in the window.

In Python: Join the event table to observation_period on person_id and where index_date between start and end; then compute prior/future days with Ibis date difference. If the person is not in any observation period on the index date, prior/future can be set to NA.

docs.title: Adding characteristics to cohort tables
docs.text: The same logic applies to cohort tables. The default index date is then cohort_start_date. You can add age (with optional age groups), sex, in-observation flag, and prior/future observation to each cohort row.

docs.title: Adding multiple characteristics at once
docs.text: PatientProfiles’ addDemographics() adds age, age group, sex, prior observation, and future observation in one go (fewer joins than adding them one by one). In Python, a single join from your table to person and observation_period, plus computed columns for age, sex, prior_observation, and future_observation, achieves the same efficiently.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Age: Compute from person’s date of birth to an index date; optionally bucket into age groups.
  • Sex: Join to person and map gender_concept_id to a label.
  • Prior/future observation: Join to observation_period, then compute days before/after the index date within that period.
  • In observation: Check whether the index date (or window) lies inside an observation period.

Implementing these with Ibis keeps everything in the database and reuses the same patterns (join person, join observation_period, then mutate).

docs.title: PatientProfiles: Summarise result
docs.text: This tutorial summarises the R PatientProfiles vignette on turning patient-level data (with demographics and intersections) into a summarised_result object using standardised estimates.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: After adding demographics (demographics), cohort intersections (cohort-intersect), concept/table intersections, and other variables to your table, you often want to summarise them into aggregate statistics in a standard format. PatientProfiles’ summariseResult() does this and produces a summarised_result (see Summarised result).

docs.title: Variable and estimate types
docs.text:
PatientProfiles maps column types (date, numeric, integer, categorical, logical) to allowed estimates:

  • Date: median, min, max, q25, q75, etc.
  • Numeric / Integer: mean, sd, median, count, percentage, etc.
  • Categorical: count, percentage.
  • Logical: count, percentage.

Not all database backends support every function (e.g. quantiles); PatientProfiles handles this so you can request the same estimates across backends.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
You specify:

  • strata – Variables to stratify by (e.g. "sex", or list("sex", "pharyngitis_before")).
  • variables – Variables to summarise (e.g. "age").
  • estimates – Which estimates (e.g. c("mean", "sd") or c("median", "q25", "q75")).
  • counts – Whether to add number of subjects/records per group.

You can omit the overall stratum with includeOverallStrata = FALSE, or add interaction strata (e.g. c("sex", "pharyngitis_before")) to get combined levels.

docs.title: Group and strata
docs.text:
The summarised_result model has two aggregation levels:

  • Group – High-level (e.g. cohort name); set with group = "cohort_name", includeOverallGroup = FALSE if you only want per-cohort.
  • Strata – Stratification (e.g. sex, age group); set with strata and includeOverallStrata.

You can specify different variables and estimates per variable group using lists, e.g. numeric variables with median/q25/q75 and categorical with count/percentage.

docs.title: In Python
docs.text:
There is no PatientProfiles port yet. You can:

  1. Build the same summaries with Ibis (or pandas): group by strata, then aggregate (mean, sd, count, etc.).
  2. Shape the result into the summarised_result column layout (result_id, cdm_name, group_name, group_level, strata_name, strata_level, variable_name, variable_level, estimate_name, estimate_type, estimate_value, additional_name, additional_level).
  3. Attach a settings table (result_type, package_name, package_version, etc.) and use the same suppression/export rules as in R.

This keeps your Python outputs compatible with R packages (CohortCharacteristics, visOmopResults) that consume summarised_result.

docs.title: PatientProfiles: Table intersections
docs.text: This tutorial summarises the R PatientProfiles vignette on adding variables based on any OMOP table (or a subset): did the person have a record in that table in a given window? How many? When? Days to that record?

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Besides cohort and concept intersections, PatientProfiles lets you add table intersections: variables derived from an arbitrary table (e.g. visit_occurrence, drug_exposure, or a filtered/computed table) relative to an index date and window.

The same four patterns apply:

  • addTableIntersectFlag – Any record in the table (in the window)?
  • addTableIntersectCount – Count of records in the window.
  • addTableIntersectDate – First or last record date in the window.
  • addTableIntersectDays – Days from index date to that date.

docs.title: Target start/end columns
docs.text:
The table has a “record” date or an interval:

  • targetStartDate and targetEndDate the same (e.g. condition_start_date): you are counting events (rows) whose date falls in the window.
  • targetStartDate and targetEndDate different (e.g. drug_exposure_start_date, drug_exposure_end_date): you are counting overlapping intervals: “was there an ongoing exposure in the window?”

So for drug_exposure, “had drug in 30 days before index” can mean either “at least one drug start in that window” or “at least one drug spell overlapping that window”, depending on which columns you use.

docs.title: Subset tables
docs.text: You can pass a subset of a table (e.g. drug_exposure filtered to one ingredient, or a union of procedure_occurrence and measurement with a common record_date). That table must be in the CDM (or write schema) and have person_id plus the start/end (or single date) columns you use. PatientProfiles’ table-intersect functions work on whatever table you point to; in Python you’d use an Ibis expression that’s already filtered or a materialised table in the write schema.

docs.title: In Python
docs.text:
With CDMConnector and Ibis:

  1. Take the target table (or filtered/union expression), join to your cohort/table on person_id.
  2. Filter so that either the record date or the [start, end] interval overlaps your window relative to the index date.
  3. Aggregate: any → flag, count → count; min/max date → first/last; compute days from index.

This gives you table-based flag, count, date, and days. Use the same naming and summarised_result layout as for cohort/concept intersections so downstream table/plot tools (CohortCharacteristics, visOmopResults) can consume the results.

docs.title: Re-exporting functions from omopgenerics
docs.text: omopgenerics (R) is intended mainly for developers; end users of the OHDSI/DARWIN ecosystem typically use it indirectly through packages like CDMConnector, PatientProfiles, etc. So that users do not need to attach omopgenerics explicitly, those packages re-export the functions that users need. This tutorial lists what is typically re-exported so that a Python package (e.g. CDMConnector) can expose an equivalent surface.

docs.title: CDM reference
docs.text:
If your package creates cdm_reference objects (e.g. via cdm_from_con or cdm_from_tables), re-export (or provide) at least:

  • cdm_name / cdm_version / cdm_con – Access CDM metadata and connection.
  • tableSource / tableName / cdmReference – Access table-level metadata (if your object model has them).
  • insert_table / drop_table / list_source_tables / read_source_table – Modify and query the CDM.
  • omop_columns / omop_tables / cohort_columns – Schema helpers for building valid tables.

In Python CDMConnector, these are already part of the main package (cdmconnector.cdm, cdmconnector.schemas).

docs.title: Cohorts
docs.text:
If your package creates cohort_table objects, re-export (or provide):

  • settings (cohort set) / attrition / cohort_count – Read cohort metadata.
  • cohort_codelist – If you support codelists attached to cohorts.
  • bind – Combine cohort tables.

In Python CDMConnector, cohort_count, attrition, record_cohort_attrition, bind, new_cohort_table, generate_cohort_set, generate_concept_cohort_set, and read_cohort_set are in cdmconnector.cohorts.

docs.title: Summarised result
docs.text:
If your package creates or consumes summarised_result objects, re-export (or provide):

  • suppress – Minimum cell count suppression.
  • bind – Combine summarised results.
  • settings – Read result settings.
  • export_summarised_result / import_summarised_result – Export/import (e.g. CSV).
  • group_columns / strata_columns / additional_columns – Column name helpers for the standard format.

In Python, summarised result support may be added in a separate module or package; the same functions should be exposed so that scripts can use one package for both CDM access and result handling.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Re-exporting (or implementing) these functions in your package gives users a single entry point (e.g. import cdmconnector as cdm) and keeps the API aligned with the R omopgenerics ecosystem for documentation and cross-language consistency.

docs.title: Summarised result
docs.text: A summarised result is a standard format for storing aggregated summary statistics (no patient-level data). It is used across the OHDSI/DARWIN ecosystem (e.g. in R omopgenerics and packages like PatientProfiles, IncidencePrevalence). This tutorial describes the structure so you can produce or consume summarised results in Python when the format is adopted.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
A summarised result consists of two parts:

  1. Results table – One row per estimate, with columns identifying the group, strata, variable, and estimate.
  2. Settings table – One row per result_id with the settings used to generate that set of results.

docs.title: Results table columns
docs.text:
Typical columns (names may vary slightly):

Column Type Description result_id integer Links to settings; groups rows with the same parameters. cdm_name character Name of the CDM used. group_name character Names of grouping variables (e.g. cohort_name), separated by &&&. group_level character Corresponding levels, separated by &&&. strata_name character Names of stratification variables (e.g. sex, age_group). strata_level character Corresponding levels. variable_name character Name of the variable (e.g. "Age group"). variable_level character Level of the variable (e.g. "10 to 50"); can be NA. estimate_name character Name of the estimate (e.g. "count", "mean"). estimate_type character Type: e.g. "integer", "numeric". estimate_value character Value as string (all values stored as character). additional_name character Extra grouping names. additional_level character Extra grouping levels.
  • group_name / group_level – High-level aggregation (e.g. cohort, codelist).
  • strata_name / strata_level – Stratification (e.g. age group, sex).
  • additional_name / additional_level – Any further grouping.

docs.title: Settings table
docs.text:
• One row per result_id.
  • At least: result_type (e.g. name of the function that produced the results), package_name, package_version.
  • Any extra parameters (e.g. study name, analysis settings) can be stored as additional columns.

docs.title: Creating a summarised result
docs.text: In R omopgenerics, newSummarisedResult(x, settings) creates a summarised_result from a results table and an optional settings table. In Python, when this format is supported, you would pass a DataFrame (or Arrow table) with the required columns and an optional settings DataFrame.

docs.title: Combining summarised results
docs.text: Multiple summarised result objects can be combined with bind. Result IDs are assigned so that results with the same settings are merged into one group. In R: bind(result1, result2).

docs.title: Minimum cell count suppression
docs.text: To avoid re-identification, counts below a threshold (e.g. 5) are often suppressed. See Suppression. When exporting, suppression is typically applied automatically (e.g. exportSummarisedResult in R).

docs.title: Export and import
docs.text: Summarised results are often exported as CSV (results + settings). In R: exportSummarisedResult(result, path), importSummarisedResult(path). The export usually includes suppression. In Python, you can implement equivalent export/import using the same column names and rules.

docs.title: Tidy format
docs.text:
For easier filtering and plotting, the “tidy” format:

  • Splits group, strata, and additional name-level pairs into separate columns.
  • Adds settings columns to the results.
  • Pivots estimates so each estimate_name becomes a column.

This loses the strict standardised column set but makes analysis more convenient. In R this is done with tidy(result). In Python, you can replicate this with pandas: split the &&&-separated columns, merge in settings, and pivot estimate_name/estimate_value.

docs.title: Utility functions (R omopgenerics)
docs.text:
• settingsColumns(), groupColumns(), strataColumns(), additionalColumns() – Return the names of settings/group/strata/additional columns.
  • filterSettings(), filterStrata(), etc. – Filter the summarised_result by settings or by group/strata/additional levels without converting to tidy.
  • splitGroup(), splitStrata(), splitAdditional() – Split name-level pairs into separate columns.
  • uniteGroup(), uniteStrata(), uniteAdditional() – Build name-level pairs from separate columns (useful when creating summarised results from custom analyses).

When implementing summarised results in Python, mirroring these helpers will improve interoperability with R packages and documentation.

docs.title: Suppression of summarised results
docs.text: Minimum cell count suppression is an important step in many studies to reduce the risk of re-identification. Thresholds vary by data source; a common choice is to suppress counts below 5. This tutorial explains how suppression works for summarised result objects in the omopgenerics (R) ecosystem, so you can implement or use the same rules in Python.

docs.title: When is a record suppressed?
docs.text:
A record is suppressed if all of the following hold:

  1. estimate_name contains the word "count" (e.g. "count", "outcome_count", "count_of_individuals").
  2. estimate_type is "numeric" or "integer".
  3. The numeric estimate_value is strictly between 0 and the minimum cell count (e.g. < 5 and > 0).

The record is not removed; estimate_value is replaced with a placeholder such as "<5" (or "<{minCellCount}").

docs.title: Linked suppression
docs.text: Suppressing one count can trigger suppression of other linked estimates.

docs.title: Group-level suppression
docs.text: If the suppressed estimate has variable_name equal to "number records" or "number subjects" (case-insensitive), the entire group of records is suppressed. A group is defined by the same: result_id, cdm_name, group_name, group_level, strata_name, strata_level, additional_name, additional_level. This is used to suppress all demographics for a cohort when the cohort size is below the threshold. Linked estimates in the same group are typically set to "-".

docs.title: Variable-level suppression
